entries.text: L. who has now read to the end of 1914, still thinks it extraordinarily good: very strange: very interesting: very sad. We discussed my sadness. But my difficulty is this: I cannot bring myself to believe that he is right. It may be simply that I exaggerated its badness, and therefore he now, finding it not so bad, exaggerates its goodness. If it is to be published, I must at once sit down and correct: how can I? Every other sentence seemed to me bad. But I am shelving the question till he has done, which should be tonight.

to prove that both things I had written down were false, a proof that seems almost impossible, Löwy came yesterday evening himself and interrupted me in my writing

 Karl’s habit of repeating everything in the same tone of voice. He tells someone a story from his business admittedly not with so many details that they would in themselves finish off the story, but nonetheless in a slow manner, thorough only due to that, as a communication that isn’t trying to be anything else and is therefore also done with upon its completion. A little while passes with another matter, he unexpectedly finds a transition to his story and pulls it out again in its old form, almost without any addition but also almost without any omission, with the harmlessness of a person who carries around the room a ribbon attached treacherously to his back. Now my parents are especially fond of him, hence feel his habits more strongly than they notice them—and so it happens that they, particularly my mother, unconsciously give him the opportunity for repetitions. If one evening the moment for the repetition of a story won’t quite come, then my mother is there, she asks and indeed with a curiosity that doesn’t cease even after the question has been asked, as one would expect. And even after several evenings my mother positively chases with her questions after stories that have already been repeated and could no longer come by themselves. But Karl’s habit reigns so powerfully that it often has the strength to justify itself completely. No one ends up with such regular frequency in a position to tell individual family members a story that basically concerns all of them. The family circle, which in such cases grows slowly at intervals always only by one person, must then be told the story almost as often as people are there. And because I’m the only one who has recognized Karl’s habit, I’m also usually the one who hears the story first and whom the repetitions give only the slight pleasure of confirming an observation.

 Envy of a supposed success of Baum’s whom I’m actually so fond of. Here the feeling of having in the middle of my body a ball of yarn, which quickly winds itself up with countless threads that it stretches from the edge of my body to itself.

 Löwy—My father about him: He who lies down in bed with dogs gets up with bugs. I couldn’t contain myself and said something disordered. My father’s especially calm reply (though after a long pause that was otherwise filled): “You know that I must not get agitated and must be treated with care. Such talk from you is the last thing I need. I have enough agitation right now, quite enough. So don’t bother me with such things.” I say: “I’m trying hard to restrain myself” and feel in my father as always at such extreme moments the existence of a wisdom of which I can grasp only a breath.

 Death of Löwy’s grandfather, a man who had an open hand, knew several languages, had taken long journeys deep into Russia and who once refused to eat on a Saturday at the home of a wonder rabbi in Yekaterinoslav because the long hair and a colorful neckerchief of that rabbi’s son made the piety of the house suspicious to him.—The bed was set up in the middle of the room, the candleholders of friends and relatives were borrowed, the room thus full of light and smoke of the candles. About 40 men stood around his bed all day to take heart from the dying of a pious man. He was conscious until his end and at the right moment with his hand on his breast began to recite the prayers meant for this time. During his suffering and after his death the grandmother, who was with the women gathered in the next room wept incessantly but during the dying she was completely calm because it is a commandment to ease the dying man’s death to the best of one’s ability. With his own prayers he departed. For this death after so pious a life he was much envied.

 Pesach festival. An association of rich Jews rents a bakery, its members take over all the tasks of producing the so-called 18 minute-matzos for the heads of the families: the fetching of water, the koshering, the kneading, the cutting, the perforating.

 —

Yesterday slept after Bar-Kochba.

 from 7 on with Löwy, read aloud a letter from his father. Evening at Baum’s.

 I want to write, with a constant trembling on my forehead. I’m sitting in my room in the headquarters of the noise of the whole apartment. I hear all the doors banging, their noise spares me only the footsteps of those running between them, I hear even the slam of the oven door in the kitchen. My father bursts through the doors of my room and passes through in his dragging dressing gown, the ashes are being scraped out of the stove in the next room, Valli asks shouting into the indefinite through the hall as if through a Paris street whether Father’s hat has been cleaned yet, a hiss that wants to be my friend raises the cry of an answering voice. The apartment door is unlatched and makes a noise as if from a catarrhal throat, then continues to open with the brief singing of a woman’s voice and closes with a dull manly jerk that sounds the most inconsiderate of all. My father is gone, now begins the gentler more scattered more hopeless noise, led by the voices of the two canaries. I thought of it before, but with the canaries it occurs to me anew, whether I shouldn’t open the door a little crack, crawl like a snake into the next room and thus on the floor ask my sisters and their governess for quiet.

 The bitterness I felt yesterday evening when Max read aloud my little automobile story at Baum’s. I was isolated from everyone and in the face of the story I positively kept my chin pressed to my chest. The disordered sentences of this story with gaps through which one could stick both hands; one sentence sounds high, one sentence sounds low as the case may be; one sentence rubs against the next like the tongue against a hollow or false tooth; one sentence comes marching up with so rough a beginning that the whole story falls into morose astonishment; a sleepy imitation of Max (reproaches dulled – – spurred on) sways in, sometimes it looks like a dancing course in its first fifteen minutes. I explain it to myself by saying that I don’t have enough time and peace to bring up from my depths the possibilities of my talent in their entirety. Therefore it’s always only broken-off beginnings that come to the surface, broken-off beginnings, for example, through the whole automobile story. If I were ever able to write a larger whole well formed from beginning to end, then the story would never be able to detach itself from me definitively and I might listen calmly and with open eyes as a blood relative of a healthy story to a reading of it, but as it is every little piece of the story wanders homelessly and drives me in the opposite direction.—At the same time I can still be glad if this explanation is correct.

 Performance of Bar-Kochba by Goldfaden.

 False judgment of the play in the whole hall and on the stage. I had brought along a bouquet for Frau Tschissik with an attached visiting card inscribed with gratitude and was waiting for the moment when I could have it presented to her. Now the performance had begun late, Frau Tschissik’s main scene had been promised me only for the 4 act, in impatience and fear the flowers could wilt I had the waiter unwrap the flowers during the 3rd act (it was 11 o’clock), now they lay sideways on a table, the kitchen staff and several dirty regular guests handed them to each other and sniffed them, I could only watch worriedly and angrily nothing else, during her main scene in the prison I loved Fr. Tschissik and yet inwardly pressed her to finish, finally the act unnoticed by me in my distraction had come to an end, the headwaiter held out the flowers, Fr. T. took them between the closing curtains, she bowed in a small crack of the curtain and didn’t come back again. No one noticed my love and I had wanted to show it to everyone and in that way make it valuable to Fr. T., they’d hardly even noticed the bouquet. Meanwhile it was already past 12 everyone was tired, several spectators had left early, I’d had the urge to throw my glass at them.—With me was the controller Pokorny from our institute a Christian. He, whom I usually like, bothered me. My concern was the flowers, not his affairs. At the same time I knew that he understood the play poorly, while I had no time, desire or ability to impose on him help he didn’t think he needed. Finally I felt ashamed before him that I myself was paying such poor attention. He also bothered me in intercourse with Max and even by the memory that I liked him before, would like him again afterward and that he could take my behavior today amiss.—But I wasn’t the only one bothered in this way. Max felt responsible because of his laudatory article in the newspaper. It was too late for the Jews accompanied by Bergmann. The members of the Bar-Kochba Association had come because of the name of the play and were bound to be disappointed. Since I’m acquainted with Bar-Kochba only from this play, I wouldn’t have given any association that name. Two shopgirls in their whores’ evening dresses were in the back of the hall with their lovers and had to be silenced by loud shouts during death scenes. Finally people on the street banged on the large windows in annoyance that they saw so little of the stage.

 On the stage the Klugs were missing. Ridiculous extras. “Coarse Jews” as Löwy said. Traveling salesmen who weren’t even paid. Most of the time they were concerned only with concealing or enjoying their laughter, even if they otherwise meant well. A round-cheeked man with a blond beard, at the sight of whom one could hardly contain one’s laughter, looked especially funny when he laughed due to the unnaturalness of the stuck-on shaking full beard which during his certainly unintended laughter bordered his cheeks incorrectly. Another man laughed only when he wanted to, but then a lot. When Löwy died singing, writhed in the arms of these two elders, was supposed to slide slowly to earth with the fading song, they put their heads together behind his back to at last be able to laugh their fill unseen by the audience (as they believed). Even yesterday when I remembered this at lunch I couldn’t help laughing.—In prison Fr. Tschissik has to take the helmet off the drunken Roman governor (the young Pipes) visiting her and put it on herself. When she takes it off, a crumpled towel falls out, which Pipes apparently stuffed in because the helmet pinched him too much. Yet even though he must have known that the helmet would be taken off him on stage, he looks reproachfully at Frau Tschissik forgetting his drunkenness.—Beautiful things: how Frau T. writhed under the hands of the Roman soldiers (though she first had to pull them to her, for they were apparently afraid to touch her), while due to her care and art the movements of the three people almost only almost followed the rhythm of the singing; the song in which she heralds the appearance of the Messiah and manages only due to her power not to spoil the effect when she represents harp playing by movements of violin bowing; in the prison, where at the frequent approach of footsteps she breaks off her song of mourning, hurries to the treadmill and turns it while singing a work song, then runs off again to her song and again to the mill, how she sings in her sleep when Papus visits her, and her mouth is open like a squinting eye, how in general the corners of her mouth when opening are reminiscent of the corners of the eyes.—In the white veil, as in the black she was beautiful.—Newly recognized movements of hers: Pressing her hand deep into her not very good bodice, brief jerk of her shoulders and hips in scorn, especially when turning her back to the one scorned.—She led the whole performance like the mother of a family. She prompted everyone, but never faltered herself; she instructed the extras, implored them finally pushed them if necessary; when she wasn’t on stage, her clear voice mingled with the weak choral singing on stage; she held the folding screen (which in the last act was supposed to represent a citadel), which the extras would have knocked over ten times.—I had hoped to satisfy my love for her a little with the bouquet, it was completely useless. It’s possible only through literature or through sleeping together. I write this not because I didn’t know it, but because it’s perhaps good to write down warnings often.

In the afternoon a letter to Erna, looked through a story Der blinde Gast by Pick and made corrections, read a little Strindberg, then didn’t sleep, at home at ½ past 8, back at 10 o’clock, out of fear of headaches, which are already beginning, and because I had also slept only very little at night, didn’t do any more work, partly also because I was afraid of spoiling a tolerable passage written yesterday. The fourth day since August when I have written nothing at all. The letters are to blame, I will try to write none at all or only very short letters. How bashful I now am too and how it flings me around! Yesterday evening the overjoyed state after I had read several lines of Jammes, with whom I otherwise have nothing to do, but whose French, it’s about a visit with a poet friend had so strong an effect on me.

(Continuation of the text from this page)

 the slightest effort necessary for it. But the inspector too always entered the station with an expression as if this time he would definitely uncover my mismanagement. He always pushed the door of the hut open with his knee while looking at me. No sooner had he opened my book than he found a mistake. It took me a long time to prove to him by recalculating before his eyes that it was not I but he who had made a mistake. He was always dissatisfied with my receipts, then he slapped the book and looked at me sharply again. “We will have to shut down the railroad” he said every time. “It will come to that” I usually replied.

 After the audit was completed, our relationship changed. I had always prepared schnapps and whenever possible some delicacy. We drank to each other, he sang with a tolerable voice, but always only two songs, one was sad and began: Where are you going little child in the forest?, the second was merry and began like this: “Boon companions, I’m one of you!” Subject to the mood I was able to put him in, I was paid my wage in installments. But only at the beginning of such amusements did I observe him with any intention, later we were completely united, shamelessly reviled the management, I received secret promises whispered in my ear about the career he would attain for me and finally we fell down together on the plank bed in an embrace that we often didn’t release for ten hours. The next morning he departed as my superior again. I stood before the train and saluted he usually turned around to face me once more while boarding and said: “Well, little friend, we’ll meet again in a month. You know what is at stake for you.” I can still see his puffy face turned with difficulty toward me, everything in this face pressed forward, the cheeks, the nose, the lips.

 This was the one great diversion of the month, when I let myself go; if some schnapps had been left by mistake, then I drank it up immediately after the inspector’s departure, usually I could hear the train’s departure signal even as it gurgled into me. The thirst after such a night was terrible; it was as if there were another person inside me, sticking his head and neck out of my mouth and screaming for something to drink. The inspector was provided for, he always carried a large supply of drinks on his train, but I was reliant on whatever was left over.

 But then I drank nothing all month nor did I smoke, I did my work and wanted nothing more. It was as I mentioned not much work but I did it thoroughly. One of my duties, for example, was to clean and inspect the tracks daily for a kilometer to the right and to the left of the station. But I didn’t keep to this requirement and often went much farther, so far that I could just barely see the station. In clear weather this was still possible at a distance of about 5 km, for the country was completely flat. When I was so far that the hut in the distance was almost only flickering before my eyes, I sometimes saw, my eyes deceiving me, many black dots moving toward the hut. They were whole crowds, whole troops. Sometimes, however, someone really came, then I ran, swinging my pickax, the whole long stretch back.

 Toward evening I was finished with my work and withdrew for the night into my hut. Usually no visitors came at this time, for the journey back to the villages was not entirely safe at night. Various riffraff roved the area, but they weren’t natives, they moved on to other places too, though they also came back. I got to see most of them, the secluded station attracted them, they were not actually dangerous, but one had to deal sternly with them.

 They were the only ones who disturbed me during the long twilight hours. Otherwise I lay on the plank bed, didn’t think about the past, didn’t think about the railroad, the next train didn’t come through until between 10 and 11 o’clock in the evening, in short I thought about nothing at all. Now and then I read an old newspaper that someone had thrown to me from the train, it contained scandalous stories from Kalda, which would have interested me but which I couldn’t understand from the single issue alone. Moreover, in every issue there was an installment of a novel called “The Revenge of the Commander.” I once dreamed of this commander, who always wore a dagger at his side, on a particular occasion he even held it between his teeth. Apart from that I could not read much, since it grew dark early and kerosene or a tallow candle was prohibitively expensive. For the month the railroad provided me with only ½ a liter of kerosene, which I had used up long before the month elapsed merely to keep the signal light for the train burning for ½ an hour in the evening. But this light was not even necessary and later at least on moonlit nights I no longer lit it at all. I foresaw quite correctly that after the summer elapsed I would be in desperate need of the kerosene. I therefore dug a hole in a corner of the hut, put an old small beer barrel in it and every month poured in the kerosene I had saved. The whole thing was covered up with straw and no one noticed anything. The more it stank of kerosene in the hut, the more satisfied I was; the stench became so great because it was a barrel made of old cracked wood that soaked up the kerosene. As a precaution I later buried the barrel outside the hut, for the inspector once flaunted a box of wax matches before me and, when I asked for them, threw them one after another burning into the air. Both of us and especially the kerosene were in real danger, I saved everything by choking him until he dropped all the matches.

 In my free hours I often thought about how I could prepare for the winter. If I was freezing even now in the warm season—and it was, they said, warmer than it had been in many years—I would do very badly in winter. My hoarding of kerosene was only a whim, I should have had the good sense to stock up on all sorts of things for the winter; there was no doubt that the company would not take particular interest in my welfare, but I was too careless or rather I wasn’t careless but I mattered too little to myself to have wanted to make much effort in this respect. Now in the warm season I was doing tolerably well, I left things as they were and undertook nothing further.

 One of the temptations that had brought me to this station had been the prospect of hunting. I had been told that it was a region extraordinarily rich in game and I had already secured a rifle for myself that I wanted to have sent to me when I had saved some money. Now it turned out that there was no trace of huntable game here, only wolves and bears were said to be found here, in the first months I saw none, and apart from that there were peculiar large rats here, which I was immediately able to observe running across the steppe in droves as if blown by the wind. But the game I had been looking forward to was not there. People had not misinformed me, the region rich in game existed, only it was a three days’ journey away,—I hadn’t considered that the locations in these lands, which were uninhabited for hundreds of km, must necessarily be uncertain. In any case, for the time being I didn’t need the rifle and could use the money for other things; for the winter, to be sure, I had to buy a rifle and I put aside money for it regularly. For the rats, which sometimes attacked my food my long knife sufficed. In the early days when I was still taking everything in with curiosity, I once speared one of these rats and held it in front of me at eye level against the wall. You see small animals clearly only when you have them in front of you at eye level; when you bend down toward them on the ground and look at them there, you get a false incomplete picture of them. The most striking thing about these rats was the claws, large, a little cupped and yet pointed at the end; they were very well suited to digging. Hanging in front of me against the wall in its final throes, the rat then spread its claws out straight seemingly against its living nature, they resembled a little hand reaching out toward you. In general these animals didn’t bother me much, only at night they sometimes woke me when they hurried past the hut, clattering on the hard ground as they ran. If I then sat up and perhaps lit a little wax candle, I could see somewhere in a gap under the wooden posts a rat’s feverishly working claws stuck in from outside. It was completely useless work, for to dig a sufficiently large hole for itself, it would have had to work for days and yet it fled as soon as the day brightened even a little, nonetheless it worked like a worker who knows his goal. And it did good work, they were admittedly imperceptible particles that flew up from its digging, but its claw was probably never applied without result. I often watched for a long time at night until the regularity and calm of this sight lulled me to sleep. Then I no longer had the strength to put out the little wax candle and it shone a little while longer for the rat at its work. Once on a warm night, when I again heard those claws working, I went out carefully, without lighting a candle to see the animal itself. It had lowered its head far down with its pointed snout, almost pushed it between its forelegs to get as close as possible to the wood and push its claws as deep as possible under the wood. One might have thought someone was holding on to the claws inside the hut and trying to pull the whole animal in, so tensed was everything. And yet everything was also ended with a kick, by which I struck the animal dead. Fully awake, I couldn’t allow my hut, which was my only possession, to be attacked.

 To secure the hut against these rats, I plugged all the gaps with straw and tow and every morning inspected the floor all around. I also intended to cover the floor of the hut, which until now was only hard-packed earth, with boards, which could also be useful for the winter. A peasant from the next village, named Jekoz, had promised me long ago to bring nice dry boards for this purpose, in the meantime I had also often received him hospitably for this promise, he also never stayed away for long, but came every 14 days, also sometimes had to send shipments by train, but he didn’t bring the boards. He had various excuses for this, mostly that he himself was too old to haul such a load and that his son, who would bring the boards, was presently busy working in the fields. Now Jekoz was by his own account and it also seemed to be correct far over 70 years old, but a tall, still very strong man. Besides, he also changed his excuses and another time spoke of the difficulties of acquiring such long boards as I needed. I didn’t press, I didn’t absolutely need the boards, Jekoz himself had given me the idea of covering the floor in the first place, such a covering was perhaps not even very advantageous, in short, I could listen calmly to the old man’s lies. My perpetual greeting was: “The boards, Jekoz!” Immediately the apologies began in a half-stammer, I was called inspector or captain or even just telegraphist, he promised me not only to bring the boards shortly but also, with the help of his son and several neighbors, to tear down my whole hut and build a solid house in its place. I listened until it made me tired and I pushed him out. But still standing in the doorway, seeking forgiveness, he raised his supposedly so weak arms, with which in reality he could have crushed a grown man. I knew why he didn’t bring the boards, he thought that by the time the winter was approaching, I would need the boards more desperately and would pay better, besides, as long as the boards had not been delivered, he himself would be of greater value to me. Now he was of course not stupid and knew that I was aware of his ulterior motives, but in the fact that I didn’t make use of this awareness he saw his advantage and he maintained it.

 But all the preparations I was making to secure the hut against the animals and keep myself safe for the winter had to be abandoned when—the first three months of my service were approaching their end—I fell seriously ill. Until then I had for years been spared any illness, even the slightest indisposition, this time I fell ill. It began with a heavy cough. About two hours inland from the station was a small brook from which I usually fetched my water supply in a barrel on a wheelbarrow. I also bathed there often and this cough was the consequence of it. The coughing fits were so severe that I had to double up while coughing, I believed myself unable to withstand the coughing unless I doubled up and in that way gathered all my strength. I thought the train crew would be horrified by the coughing, but they were acquainted with it, they called it the wolf’s cough. From then on I began to hear the howling in the cough. I sat on the small bench in front of the hut and greeted the train with a howl, with a howl I accompanied its departure. In the nights I knelt on the plank bed instead of lying and pressed my face into the pelts to spare myself at least the listening to the howling. I waited expectantly until the bursting of some major blood vessel would put an end to everything. But nothing of the sort happened and the cough had even gone away in a few days. But a fever remained and didn’t subside.

 This fever made me very tired, I lost all power of resistance, it could happen that sweat broke out on my forehead quite unexpectedly, then my whole body trembled and I had to lie down wherever I was and wait until my senses regathered.

Tenth Notebook

 4 (November 1914) Pepa back. Shouting, agitated, beside himself. Story of the mole that tunneled under him in the trench and that he regarded as a sign from God to move away from there. No sooner was he gone than a shot struck a soldier who had crawled after him and was now over the mole.—His captain. He could be seen clearly being taken captive. But the next day he was found naked in the woods impaled by bayonets. He probably had money on him, they had wanted to search and rob him, but he “the way officers are” had not voluntarily let himself be touched.—P. almost wept with anger and agitation when, on the way from the railroad station, he met his boss (whom he had previously revered immoderately and ridiculously) going to the theater elegantly dressed, perfumed, with opera glasses hanging around his neck. A month later he did it himself with a ticket this boss had given him. He went to “Der ungetreue Eckehart” a comedy.—Slept once in the castle of Prince Sapieha, once just in front of Austr. firing batteries, where he was lying in reserve, once in a farmhouse room, where two women slept in each of the two beds against the walls to the right and left, a girl behind the stove, and eight soldiers on the floor.—Punishment for soldiers. Stand bound to a tree until they turn blue. Because, for example, he had left my sister’s card somewhere against the rule, where it actually was lost too.—

 12 (November 1914) Parents who expect gratitude from their children (there are even those who demand it) are like usurers, they are happy to risk the capital as long as they get the interest.

 —

Have seen a great deal recently, fewer headaches. Walks with Frl. Reiss. With her at “Er und seine Schwester,” performed by Girardi. (Do you have talent, then?—Permit me to intervene and answer for you: Oh yes, oh yes) In the municipal reading room. Looked at the flag at her parents’. The 2 wonderful sisters Esther and Tilka like contrasts of glowing and dimming. Especially Tilka beautiful: olive-brown, arched lowered eyelids, the depths of Asia. Both with shawls drawn around their shoulders. They are of medium height, rather short and appear erect and tall as goddesses, one on the round pillow of the sofa, Tilka in a corner on some unrecognizable seat, perhaps on boxes. In half-sleep for a long time saw Esther, who, with the passion that according to my impression she seems to have for everything spiritual, had bitten firmly into the knot of a rope and was being swung powerfully back and forth in empty space like a bell clapper (memory of a movie poster)—The two Lieblichs. The little devilish teacher whom I also saw in half-sleep, how she flew furiously up and down in a dance, in a Cossack-like but floating dance over a slightly sloping bumpy brick pavement lying there dark brown in the twilight.

 4 (November 1915) Memory of the corner in Brescia where I distributed soldi to children on similar pavement, but in broad daylight. Memory of a church in Verona where, completely forsaken, only under the light compulsion of the duty of a pleasure traveler and under the heavy compulsion of a person lapsing into uselessness I entered reluctantly, saw a larger than life-sized dwarf stooping under the holy water basin, walked around a little, sat down and just as reluctantly went out as if outside there were another such church attached door to door.

 Recently the departure of the Jews at the state railroad station. The 2 men carrying a bag. The father loading his possessions onto his many children down to the littlest one in order to reach the platform more quickly. The strong healthy already shapeless young woman sitting on her suitcase with an infant, surrounded by acquaintances in lively conversation.

 5 (November 1915) State of excitement in the afternoon. Began with the consideration whether and how much war loan I should buy. Went to the shop twice to give the necessary instructions and returned twice without having entered. Feverishly calculated the interest. Then asked my mother to buy a 1000 K bond, but increased the amount to 2000 K. In the process it turned out that I had not even known about an investment amounting to roughly 3000 K that belonged to me and that it barely affected me at all when I found out about it. There was nothing in my head but the doubts about the war loan, which now didn’t cease for about ½ an hour on a walk through the busiest streets. I felt directly involved in the war, weighed, albeit in accordance with my knowledge, quite generally the financial prospects, raised and reduced the interest that would one day be at my disposal, etc. Gradually, however, this excitement transformed, my thoughts were steered toward writing, I felt capable of it, wanted nothing but the chance to write, considered which nights in the near future I could set aside for it, ran with heart pains across the stone bridge, felt the unhappiness, experienced so often before, of the consuming fire that was not permitted to break out, invented to express and calm myself the saying “Little friend, pour out,” sang it incessantly to a special melody and accompanied the song by repeatedly pressing and releasing a handkerchief in my pocket like a bagpipe.

 6 (November 1915) Sight of the antlike movement of the crowd in front of the trench and in it.

 At the home of Oskar Pollak’s mother. Good impression of his sister. Incidentally, is there anyone to whom I don’t bow? In the case of Grünberg, for example, who in my opinion is a very significant person and is almost universally underestimated for reasons impenetrable to me: If I were faced with the choice, say, that one of the two of us would have to perish immediately (with regard to him it is very probable, for he is said to have a far-advanced tuberculosis) but that it would depend on my decision who it should be, I would find the question ridiculous to the outermost edge of its theoretical formulation, since obviously the incomparably more valuable Grünberg would have to be preserved. Grünberg too would agree with me. In the final uncontrollable moments, to be sure, I would as anyone else would have done much earlier invent evidence in my favor, evidence that due to its crudeness, bareness, falseness would at any other time have made me vomit. These final moments, to be sure, are happening now too, when no one is imposing a choice on me, they are those when I seek to examine myself while keeping away all distracting external influences.

 “Silently the “black ones” sit around the fire. The glow of the flames flickers on their somber enthusiast faces.”

Dear Kitty,

The British have finally scored a few successes in Africa and Stalingrad hasn’t fallen yet, so the men are happy and we had coffee and tea this morning. For the rest, nothing special to report.

This week I’ve been reading a lot and doing little work. That’s the way things ought to be. That’s surely the road to success.

Mother and I are getting along better lately, but we’re never close. Father’s not very open about his feelings, but he’s the same sweetheart he’s always been. We lit the stove a few days ago and the entire room is still filled with smoke. I prefer central heating, and I’m probably not the only one. Margot’s a stinker (there’s no other word for it), a constant source of irritation, morning, noon and night.

Anne Frank

Dearest Kitty,

To take our minds off matters as well as to develop them, Father ordered a catalog from a correspondence school. Margot pored through the thick brochure three times without finding anything to her liking and within her budget. Father was easier to satisfy and decided to write and ask for a trial lesson in “Elementary Latin.” No sooner said than done. The lesson arrived, Margot set to work enthusiastically and decided to take the course, despite the expense. It’s much too hard for me, though I’d really like to learn Latin.

To give me a new project as well, Father asked Mr. Kleiman for a children’s Bible so I could finally learn something about the New Testament.

“Are you planning to give Anne a Bible for Hanukkah?” Margot asked, somewhat perturbed.

“Yes … Well, maybe St. Nicholas Day would be a better occasion,” Father replied.

Jesus and Hanukkah don’t exactly go together.

Since the vacuum cleaner’s broken, I have to take an old brush to the rug every night. The window’s closed, the light’s on, the stove’s burning, and there I am brushing away at the rug. “That’s sure to be a problem,” I thought to myself the first time. “There’re bound to be complaints.” I was right: Mother got a headache from the thick clouds of dust whirling around the room, Margot’s new Latin dictionary was caked with dirt, and Pim grumbled that the floor didn’t look any different anyway. Small thanks for my pains.

We’ve decided that from now on the stove is going to be lit at seven-thirty on Sunday mornings instead of five-thirty. I think it’s risky. What will the neighbors think of our smoking chimney?

It’s the same with the curtains. Ever since we first went into hiding, they’ve been tacked firmly to the windows. Sometimes one of the ladies or gentlemen can’t resist the urge to peek outside. The result: a storm of reproaches. The response: “Oh, nobody will notice.” That’s how every act of carelessness begins and ends. No one will notice, no one will hear, no one will pay the least bit of attention. Easy to say, but is it true?

At the moment, the tempestuous quarrels have subsided;only Dussel and the van Daans are still at loggerheads. When Dussel is talking about Mrs. van D., he invariably calls her “that old bat” or “that stupid hag,” and conversely, Mrs. van D. refers to our ever so learned gentleman as an “old maid” or a “touchy neurotic spinster,” etc.

The pot calling the kettle black!

Yours, Anne

MONDAY EVENING, NOVEMBER 8, 1943

Dearest Kitty,

If you were to read all my letters in one sitting, you’d be struck by the fact that they were written in a variety of moods. It annoys me to be so dependent on the moods here in the Annex, but I’m not the only one: we’re all subject to them. If I’m engrossed in a book, I have to rearrange my thoughts before I can mingle with other people, because otherwise they might think I was strange. As you can see, I’m currently in the middle of a depression. I couldn’t really tell you what set it off, but I think it stems from my cowardice, which confronts me at every turn. This evening, when Bep was still here, the doorbell rang long and loud. I instantly turned white, my stomach churned, and my heart beat wildly—and all because I was afraid.

At night in bed I see myself alone in a dungeon, without Father and Mother. Or I’m roaming the streets, or the Annex is on fire, or they come in the middle of the night to take us away and I crawl under my bed in desperation. I see everything as if it were actually taking place. And to think it might all happen soon!

Miep often says she envies us because we have such peace and quiet here. That may be true, but she’s obviously not thinking about our fear.

I simply can’t imagine the world will ever be normal again for us. I do talk about “after the war,” but it’s as if I were talking about a castle in the air, something that can never come true.

I see the eight of us in the Annex as if we were a patch of blue sky surrounded by menacing black clouds. The perfectly round spot on which we’re standing is still safe, but the clouds are moving in on us, and the ring between us and the approaching danger is being pulled tighter and tighter. We’re surrounded by darkness and danger, and in our desperate search for a way out we keep bumping into each other. We look at the fighting down below and the peace and beauty up above. In the meantime, we’ve been cut off by the dark mass of clouds, so that we can go neither up nor down. It looms before us like an impenetrable wall, trying to crush us, but not yet able to. I can only cry out and implore, “Oh, ring, ring, open wide and let us out!”

Yours, Anne

(Lord’s day). In the morn to our own church, where Mr. Mills did
begin to nibble at the Common Prayer, by saying “Glory be to the Father,
&c.” after he had read the two psalms; but the people had been so little
used to it, that they could not tell what to answer. This declaration of
the King’s do give the Presbyterians some satisfaction, and a pretence
to read the Common Prayer, which they would not do before because of
their former preaching against it. After dinner to Westminster, where I
went to my Lord’s, and having spoke with him, I went to the Abbey, where
the first time that ever I heard the organs in a cathedral! Thence to
my Lord’s, where I found Mr. Pierce, the surgeon, and with him and Mr.
Sheply, in our way calling at the Bell to see the seven Flanders mares
that my Lord has bought lately, where we drank several bottles of Hull
ale. Much company I found to come to her, and cannot wonder at it, for
she is very pretty and wanton. Hence to my father’s, where I found my
mother in greater and greater pain of the stone. I staid long and drank
with them, and so home and to bed. My wife seemed very pretty to-day, it
being the first time I had given her leave to wear a black patch.

In the morning, being very rainy, by coach with Sir W. Pen and
my wife to Whitehall, and sent her to Mrs. Bunt’s, and he and I to Mr.
Coventry’s about business, and so sent for her again, and all three
home again, only I to the Mitre (Mr. Rawlinson’s), where Mr. Pierce,
the Purser, had got us a most brave chine of beef, and a dish of
marrowbones. Our company my uncle Wight, Captain Lambert, one Captain
Davies, and purser Barter, Mr. Rawlinson, and ourselves; and very merry.
After dinner I took coach, and called my wife at my brother’s, where I
left her, and to the Opera, where we saw “The Bondman,” which of old we
both did so doat on, and do still; though to both our thinking not
so well acted here (having too great expectations), as formerly at
Salisbury-court. But for Betterton he is called by us both the best
actor in the world. So home by coach, I lighting by the way at my uncle
Wight’s and staid there a little, and so home after my wife, and to bed.

At the office all the morning. At noon I went by appointment to the
Sun in Fish Street to a dinner of young Mr. Bernard’s for myself, Mr.
Phillips, Davenport, Weaver, &c., where we had a most excellent dinner,
but a pie of such pleasant variety of good things, as in all my life I
never tasted. Hither came to me Captain Lambert to take his leave of me,
he being this day to set sail for the Straights. We drank his farewell
and a health to all our friends, and were very merry, and drank wine
enough. Hence to the Temple to Mr. Turner about drawing up my bill in
Chancery against T. Trice, and so to Salisbury Court, where Mrs. Turner
is come to town to-night, but very ill still of an ague, which I was
sorry to see. So to the Wardrobe and talked with my Lady, and so home
and to bed.

Lay long talking pleasantly with my wife in bed, it having rained,
and do still, very much all night long. Up and to the office, where
we sat till noon. This morning we had news by letters that Sir Richard
Stayner is dead at sea in the Mary, which is now come into Portsmouth
from Lisbon; which we are sorry for, he being a very stout seaman. But
there will be no great miss of him for all that. Dined at home with my
wife, and all the afternoon among my workmen, and at night to my office
to do business there, and then to see Sir W. Pen, who is still sick, but
his pain less than it was. He took occasion to talk with me about Sir J.
Minnes’s intention to divide the entry and the yard, and so to keep him
out of the yard, and forcing him to go through the garden to his house.
Which he is vexed at, and I am glad to see that Sir J. Minnes do use him
just as he do me, and so I perceive it is not anything extraordinary
his carriage to me in the matter of our houses, for this is worse than
anything he has done to me, that he should give order for the stopping
up of his way to his house without so much as advising with him or
letting of him know it, and I confess that it is very highly and basely
done of him. So to my office again, and after doing business there, then
home to supper and to bed.

Up and to my office, shewing myself to Sir W. Batten, and Sir J.
Minnes, and no great matter made of my periwigg, as I was afeard there
would be. Among other things there came to me Shales of Portsmouth, by
my order, and I began to discourse with him about the arrears of stores
belonging to the Victualling Office there, and by his discourse I am in
some hopes that if I can get a grant from the King of such a part of all
I discover I may chance to find a way to get something by the by, which
do greatly please me the very thoughts of. Home to dinner, and very
pleasant with my wife, who is this day also herself making of marmalett
of quince, which she now do very well herself. I left her at it and by
coach I to the New Exchange and several places to buy and bring home
things, among others a case I bought of the trunk maker’s for my
periwigg, and so home and to my office late, and among other things
wrote a letter to Will’s uncle to hasten his removal from me, and so
home to supper and to bed. This morning Captain Cocke did give me a good
account of the Guinny trade. The Queene is in a great way to recovery.
This noon came John Angier to me in a pickle, I was sad to see him,
desiring my good word for him to go a trooper to Tangier, but I
did schoole him and sent him away with good advice, but no present
encouragement. Presently after I had a letter from his poor father
at Cambridge, who is broke, it seems, and desires me to get him a
protection, or a place of employment; but, poor man, I doubt I can helpe
him, but will endeavour it.

Waked very betimes and lay long awake, my mind being so full of
business. Then up and to St. James’s, where I find Mr. Coventry full
of business, packing up for his going to sea with the Duke. Walked with
him, talking, to White Hall, where to the Duke’s lodgings, who is gone
thither to lodge lately. I appeared to the Duke, and thence Mr. Coventry
and I an hour in the Long Gallery, talking about the management of our
office, he tells me the weight of dispatch will lie chiefly on me, and
told me freely his mind touching Sir W. Batten and Sir J. Minnes, the
latter of whom, he most aptly said, was like a lapwing; that all he did
was to keepe a flutter, to keepe others from the nest that they would
find. He told me an old story of the former about the light-houses, how
just before he had certified to the Duke against the use of them, and
what a burden they are to trade, and presently after, at his being at
Harwich, comes to desire that he might have the setting one up there,
and gets the usefulness of it certified also by the Trinity House. After
long discoursing and considering all our stores and other things, as how
the King hath resolved upon Captain Taylor

 [Coventry, writing to Secretary Bennet (November 14th, 1664), refers
 to the objections made to Taylor, and adds: “Thinks the King will
 not easily consent to his rejection, as he is a man of great
 abilities and dispatch, and was formerly laid aside at Chatham on
 the Duchess of Albemarle’s earnest interposition for another. He is
 a fanatic, it is true, but all hands will be needed for the work cut
 out; there is less danger of them in harbour than at sea, and profit
 will convert most of them” (“Calendar of State Papers,” Domestic,
 1664-65, p. 68).]

and Colonell Middleton, the first to be Commissioner for Harwich and the
latter for Portsmouth, I away to the ‘Change, and there did very
much business, so home to dinner, and Mr. Duke, our Secretary for the
Fishery, dined with me. After dinner to discourse of our business, much
to my content, and then he away, and I by water among the smiths on
the other side, and to the alehouse with one and was near buying 4 or 5
anchors, and learned something worth my knowing of them, and so home
and to my office, where late, with my head very full of business, and so
away home to supper and to bed.

They sayled from midnight, and come to Greenwich about 5 o’clock
in the morning. I however lay till about 7 or 8, and so to my office, my
head a little akeing, partly for want of natural rest, partly having so
much business to do to-day, and partly from the newes I hear that one
of the little boys at my lodging is not well; and they suspect, by their
sending for plaister and fume, that it may be the plague; so I sent Mr.
Hater and W. Hewer to speake with the mother; but they returned to me,
satisfied that there is no hurt nor danger, but the boy is well, and
offers to be searched, however, I was resolved myself to abstain coming
thither for a while. Sir W. Batten and myself at the office all the
morning. At noon with him to dinner at Boreman’s, where Mr. Seymour with
us, who is a most conceited fellow and not over much in him. Here Sir W.
Batten told us (which I had not heard before) that the last sitting day
his cloake was taken from Mingo he going home to dinner, and that he was
beaten by the seamen and swears he will come to Greenwich, but no more
to the office till he can sit safe. After dinner I to the office and
there late, and much troubled to have 100 seamen all the afternoon
there, swearing below and cursing us, and breaking the glasse windows,
and swear they will pull the house down on Tuesday next. I sent word of
this to Court, but nothing will helpe it but money and a rope. Late at
night to Mr. Glanville’s there to lie for a night or two, and to bed.

(Lord’s day). Comes my taylor’s man in the morning, and brings my
vest home, and coate to wear with it, and belt, and silver-hilted sword.
So I rose and dressed myself, and I like myself mightily in it, and so
do my wife. Then, being dressed, to church; and after church pulled my
Lady Pen and Mrs. Markham into my house to dinner, and Sir J. Minnes
he got Mrs. Pegg along with him. I had a good dinner for them, and very
merry; and after dinner to the waterside, and so, it being very cold,
to White Hall, and was mighty fearfull of an ague, my vest being new and
thin, and the coat cut not to meet before upon my breast. Here I waited
in the gallery till the Council was up, and among others did speak
with Mr. Cooling, my Lord Chamberlain’s secretary, who tells me my Lord
Generall is become mighty low in all people’s opinion, and that he hath
received several slurs from the King and Duke of York. The people at
Court do see the difference between his and the Prince’s management, and
my Lord Sandwich’s. That this business which he is put upon of crying
out against the Catholiques and turning them out of all employment, will
undo him, when he comes to turn-out the officers out of the Army, and
this is a thing of his own seeking. That he is grown a drunken sot, and
drinks with nobody but Troutbecke, whom nobody else will keep company
with. Of whom he told me this story: That once the Duke of Albemarle in
his drink taking notice as of a wonder that Nan Hide should ever come to
be Duchesse of York, “Nay,” says Troutbecke, “ne’er wonder at that; for
if you will give me another bottle of wine, I will tell you as great,
if not greater, a miracle.” And what was that, but that our dirty Besse
(meaning his Duchesse) should come to be Duchesse of Albemarle? Here we
parted, and so by and by the Council rose, and out comes Sir G. Carteret
and Sir W. Coventry, and they and my Lord Bruncker and I went to Sir G.
Carteret’s lodgings, there to discourse about some money demanded by
Sir W. Warren, and having done that broke up. And Sir G. Carteret and
I alone together a while, where he shows a long letter, all in cipher,
from my Lord Sandwich to him. The contents he hath not yet found out,
but he tells me that my Lord is not sent for home, as several people
have enquired after of me. He spoke something reflecting upon me in the
business of pursers, that their present bad behaviour is what he did
foresee, and had convinced me of, and yet when it come last year to
be argued before the Duke of York I turned and said as the rest did. I
answered nothing to it, but let it go, and so to other discourse of
the ill state of things, of which all people are full of sorrow and
observation, and so parted, and then by water, landing in Southwarke,
home to the Tower, and so home, and there began to read “Potter’s
Discourse upon 1666,” which pleases me mightily, and then broke off and
to supper and to bed.

Up betimes, and by water with Sir R. Ford (who is going to
Parliament) to Westminster; and there landing at the New Exchange
stairs, I to Sir W. Coventry: and there he read over to me the Prince’s
and the Duke of Albemarle’s Narratives; wherein they are very severe
against him and our Office. But [Sir] W. Coventry do contemn them; only
that their persons and qualities are great, and so I do perceive [he] is
afeard of them, though he will not confess it. But he do say that, if he
can get out of these briars, he will never trouble himself with Princes
nor Dukes again. He finds several things in their Narratives, which are
both inconsistent and foolish, as well as untrue, especially as to what
the Duke of Albemarle avers of his knowing of the enemy’s being abroad
sooner than he says it, which [Sir] W. Coventry will shew him his own
letter against him, for I confess I do see so much, that, were I but
well possessed of what I should have in the world, I think I could
willingly retreat, and trouble myself no more with it. Thence home, and
there met Sir H. Cholmly, and he and I to the Excise Office to see what
tallies are paying, and thence back to the Old Exchange, by the way
talking of news, and he owning Sir W. Coventry, in his opinion, to be
one of the worthiest men in the nation, as I do really think he is. He
tells me he do think really that they will cut off my Lord Chancellor’s
head, the Chancellor at this day showing as much pride as is possible to
those few that venture their fortunes by coming to see him; and that the
Duke of York is troubled much, knowing that those that fling down the
Chancellor cannot stop there, but will do something to him, to prevent
his having it in his power hereafter to avenge himself and father-in-law
upon them. And this Sir H. Cholmly fears may be by divorcing the Queen
and getting another, or declaring the Duke of Monmouth legitimate; which
God forbid! He tells me he do verily believe that there will come in
an impeachment of High Treason against my Lord of Ormond; among other
things, for ordering the quartering of soldiers in Ireland on free
quarters; which, it seems, is High Treason in that country, and was one
of the things that lost the Lord Strafford his head, and the law is not
yet repealed; which, he says, was a mighty oversight of him not to
have it repealed, which he might with ease have done, or have justified
himself by an Act. From the Exchange I took a coach, and went to
Turlington, the great spectacle-maker, for advice, who dissuades me from
using old spectacles, but rather young ones, and do tell me that nothing
can wrong my eyes more than for me to use reading-glasses, which do
magnify much. Thence home, and there dined, and then abroad and left
my wife and Willett at her tailor’s, and I to White Hall, where the
Commissioners of the Treasury do not sit, and therefore I to Westminster
to the Hall, and there meeting with Col. Reames I did very cheaply by
him get copies of the Prince’s and Duke of Albemarle’s Narratives, which
they did deliver the other day to the House, of which I am mighty glad,
both for my present information and for my future satisfaction. So back
by coach, and took up my wife, and away home, and there in my chamber
all the evening among my papers and my accounts of Tangier to my great
satisfaction, and so to supper and to bed.

Up, and by coach to White Hall; and there I find the King and Duke
of York come the last night, and every body’s mouth full of my Lord
Anglesey’s suspension being sealed; which it was, it seems, yesterday;
so that he is prevented in his remedy at the Council; and, it seems, the
two new Treasurers did kiss the King’s hand this morning, brought in by
my Lord Arlington. They walked up and down together the Court this day,
and several people joyed them; but I avoided it, that I might not be
seen to look either way. This day also I hear that my Lord Ormond is
to be declared in Council no more Deputy Governor of Ireland, his
commission being expired: and the King is prevailed with to take it out
of his hands; which people do mightily admire, saying that he is the
greatest subject of any prince in Christendome, and hath more acres of
land than any, and hath done more for his Prince than ever any yet did.
But all will not do; he must down, it seems, the Duke of Buckingham
carrying all before him. But that, that troubles me most is, that
they begin to talk that the Duke of York’s regiment is ordered to be
disbanded; and more, that undoubtedly his Admiralty will follow: which
do shake me mightily, and I fear will have ill consequences in the
nation, for these counsels are very mad. The Duke of York do, by all
men’s report, carry himself wonderfull submissive to the King, in the
most humble manner in the world; but yet, it seems, nothing must be
spared that tends to, the keeping out of the Chancellor; and that is
the reason of all this. The great discourse now is, that the Parliament
shall be dissolved and another called, which shall give the King the
Deane and Chapter lands; and that will put him out of debt. And it
is said that Buckingham do knownly meet daily with Wildman and other
Commonwealth-men; and that when he is with them, he makes the King
believe that he is with his wenches; and something looks like the
Parliament’s being dissolved, by Harry Brouncker’s being now come back,
and appears this day the first day at White Hall; but hath not been yet
with the King, but is secure that he shall be well received, I hear. God
bless us, when such men as he shall be restored! But that, that pleases
me most is, that several do tell me that Pen is to be removed; and
others, that he hath resigned his place; and particularly Spragg tells
me for certain that he hath resigned it, and is become a partner with
Gawden in the Victualling: in which I think he hath done a very cunning
thing; but I am sure I am glad of it; and it will be well for the King
to have him out of this Office. Thence by coach, doing several errands,
home and there to dinner, and then to the Office, where all the
afternoon till late at night, and so home. Deb. hath been abroad to-day
with her friends, poor girle, I believe toward the getting of a place.
This day a boy is sent me out of the country from Impington by my cozen
Roger Pepys’ getting, whom I visited this morning at his chamber in the
Strand and carried him to Westminster Hall, where I took a turn or
two with him and Sir John Talbot, who talks mighty high for my Lord of
Ormond: and I perceive this family of the Talbots hath been raised by my
Lord. When I come home to-night I find Deb. not come home, and do doubt
whether she be not quite gone or no, but my wife is silent to me in it,
and I to her, but fell to other discourse, and indeed am well satisfied
that my house will never be at peace between my wife and I unless I let
her go, though it grieves me to the heart. My wife and I spent much time
this evening talking of our being put out of the Office, and my going to
live at Deptford at her brother’s, till I can clear my accounts, and rid
my hands of the town, which will take me a year or more, and I do think
it will be best for me to do so, in order to our living cheap, and out
of sight.

To studio too early in morning. Fell asleep on the floor. Very uncomfortable.

Compiled tape of samplable bits for Russell.

In the afternoon Mark Johnston and Christina Barchi called to enlist our support for the Saro-Wiwa appeal. Me reluctant to condemn Shell automatically. Heated argument.*

In the evening Anthea and I talked for ages and thought of a new game: each trying to guess which members of the opposite sex the other found really attractive. It took quite a long time to define the terms: I mean, should it be Spud-u-like, or Spud-u-love, or Spud-u-adore-and-settle-down-with? We finally decided it should be Spud-u’d-like-to-be-on-a-desert-island-with-for-a-week. We both ended up with very short lists. I put Matthew Evans and Arie de Geus (she doesn’t know him) on hers, and she added David and then we finally setted for Laraaji, a very good choice. On mine there was Ali, Botero’s daughter Lana(?). Since then I added James Neal’s wife.

We were also talking about the difference in upbringing behaviour between those who see themselves as gene-transmitters and those who think they’re meme-transmitters. We concluded that the former would be inclined to have more kids and invest less in them culturally. We wondered what was the best balance.

Bono in an interview: ‘A lot of English bands went to art school. We went to Brian.’ Flattering to think of myself as a sort of one-man version of the art-school experience.

Overheard Unlikelihoods series: Two stout men walking along Portobello. One to the other: ‘She was very impressed by me with all my knowledge of rugby.’

There was a problem with the plane to LA—it was stuck on the runway for three hours. Victor was also on the plane going out to California for a couple of weeks, but he was back in tourist. I read John Kobal’s Rita Hayworth book and loved it.

Cab to Century City to meet Kareem Abdul-Jabbar. His manager was good-looking and had a bull’s hat, like a cowboy hat but 100 times bigger. Kareem was so big, I could walk through his legs. He was fun and he was easy to photograph, the way I guess Negroes are. Everybody always forgets the ball, though, and somebody had to go get one.

Went to the Beverly Wilshire. They were going to put me in the old part but I wanted the new. Called New York. Nelson Lyon called. Don Simon whose wife is dying of cancer in Texas called, and Fred invited him for dinner.

Went over to the Polo Lounge.
After we saw Platinum we picked up a limo outside the theater ($15) and guess who was our driver! The catnapper!! The one from a couple of weeks ago!

Chris woke me at 7:15 to turn on the TV to the space shuttle, so I did and they stopped it after thirty-one seconds. They had oil clogged in the valves and so they’re not going up for a week at least.

Then we went to our new building on 33rd and Madison ($4). It was a beautiful sunny day and on the way up all the buildings looked great in the sunlight. Made two calls ($.20). We got to our building and it was just sensational, so beautiful, you just can’t believe it. And the neighborhood has everything, all coffee shops, all the homemade Puerto Rican kind of food that Ronnie and Robyn like, and Jean DeNoyer is opening a new restaurant, La Coupole, on 32nd Street, and the whole area is great. There’s a beautiful hotel across the street with whores that they’re trying to get out. We went into the building and the best thing is the roof terrace, it’s like a terrace for a beautiful, glamorous apartment.

We left the hotel to catch the 8:45 flight. Had some tea ($12). You can’t tip people. Everybody tells on everybody. Then we found out that if you give them a couple of cigarettes, that’s what they really want. Should have done things like that, but we hadn’t figured it out. So got to the airport and sat around for hours. Then a lady and her husband had lost their passport and for an hour and a half they were going through everything looking for it, and the lady was screaming at her husband, it was just such a movie scene, and then two minutes before the plane was ready to leave, the lady put her hand in her pocket and found it. They were really old, it was sad. It was so horrible. They were old and they couldn’t get out of China. “Where is it?” “You had it last.”

Ran into one of those English groups, like maybe the Clash, on the elevator. On the same floor as us.

Went to meet Alba Clemente, the beautiful wife of Francesco Clemente at their loft in the Tower Records building. She studied acting, she has a great laugh, and she’s rich. They live in India six months a year. That’s why his paintings look the way they do, I guess. Then we went to the Odeon (cab $10). It was fun, we chit-chatted about art. There were big silences, though. Jean Michel is so hard to talk to. His thing is he’s in love with waitresses, so he gets quiet and watches them. Alba said that her girl who was minding the children had a crush on him (lunch $90). And then we went back to her place so that Jean Michel could meet the girl, Monica, but she’d taken the kids out. And then Jean Michel was getting inspired from seeing Clemente’s work and wanted to go do some painting himself.

So we went to the studio (cab $3.50) and worked two hours. Jean Michel was painting back in the images he’d painted out when he was on smack and he came up with some masterpieces. Then he called the girl, Monica, and invited her to dinner. She wanted to go to the Lone Star because her semi-boyfriend who’s Schnabel’s assistant was going to be there, but Jean Michel didn’t want to go there because he was afraid if there was competition that he would lose the fuck.

entries.place: 绍兴会馆，北京宣武门外
entries.text: 晴，风。晚杨莘士介绍衣工吴姓者来，付裘令制，并先与银一元。得二弟信，三十日发。收本日《平报》一分。

entries.place: 绍兴会馆，北京宣武门外
entries.text:
晴。上午得二弟信，三十日發(81)。寄二弟信(八十二)。上午同許季上赴孫冠華家吊。下午出江西振捐
[
註 19
]
〔1〕一元。夜大風。

entries.place: 绍兴会馆，北京宣武门外
entries.text: 昙。晨铭伯先生来。从季市假银百。下午雨。寄钱稻〔孙〕信。晚往池田医院。夜寄和荪信。

entries.place: 绍兴会馆，北京宣武门外
entries.text: 晴。晨富华阁持拓本来。下午宋迈来笺并《藤阴杂记》二部，每部二册。夜常毅葴来。

entries.place: 绍兴会馆，北京宣武门外
entries.text: 晴。下午同徐吉轩往八道弯会罗姓并中人等，交与泉一千三百五十，收房屋讫。晚得李遐卿信。
晴。上午得胡愈之信。午后往图书分馆访宋子佩。往留黎厂买《清内府所藏唐宋元名迹》景印本一册，一元二角。下午复宫竹心信。
昙。星期休息。上午母亲令人持来书二部，鸭肝一碗，花生一合。午后寄朱可铭信。寄三弟信。下午微雨。夜濯足。
晴。上午得胡萍霞信。午后往世界语校讲。
晴。上午往中大讲。往山本医院诊。夜素园、季野来。
晴，风。上午寄漱园信并《坟》之序目，附致小峰信，又附振铎来信之半。下午收十月分薪水泉四百。得景宋信，十月卅日发。
晴。上午得易寅村信。元庆来。得霁野所寄《莽原》。得淑卿所寄《语丝》。下午雨。晚衣萍、小峰、漱六来。夜出街，买《日本童话选集》一本，三元四角。
星期。昙。上午江绍原来。得小峰信并泉百。
昙。午后得杨律师信。晚得小峰信并书籍、杂志。夜康农、修甫、友松来。康农赠孩子衣帽各一。雨。
晴。下午寄紫佩信。寄杨律师信。
晴。上午收九月分编辑费三百。得钦文信并代买《青在堂梅谱》一本，价二元。得诗荃所寄《Graphik der Ne-uzeit》一本，照相二张，自作铜版画一枚。午后往内山书店买《书道全集》(一)、《昆虫记》各一本，共泉五元。夜译《亚克与人性》毕，共八千字。

现在一天大部分时间，都在无聊地上班。倘若不记，这一天也实在没有什么可记，记起来又觉得很单调，真没办法。无已，还是记罢——

今晨仍是机械似的上班。

过午体育打篮球。

吹了一天风，晚来天气有点冷了。

我向上海璧恒公司预定的《歌德全集》，计算着早该来了，然而一直到现在不见到。我每天上班回来，看见桌上没有信，真颇有点惘然之感呢。

今天又托图书馆买了两本书，一是Herbert Read的 Phases of English Poetry ， 一是Robert Graves 的。

今天同虎文约定，他来看我。从早晨就在屋里等他，只是不见他来。到了晚上，快熄灯的时候，才从工友手里看到他的名片——他来了，竟然没见到我，同来者还有杨丙辰先生。我不能写出我是怎样的抱歉!立刻写给他一封信。

今天读的书仍然是philology和Loci Critici 。

晚上同长之谈话，谈到我写文章的困难。真的，我为什么把写文章看作那样一种困难痛苦的工作，许多好好的意念，都在想写而不写之间空空跑过了。
上课。秋暮矣，感而有赋，填一词记之，未成。阅昨日之N. Y. Times 报，论土耳其事。

二四、理想贵有统系

吾近来所以不惮烦而琐琐记吾所持见解者，盖有故焉。吾人平日读书虽多，思想虽杂，而不能有有统系的理想，不能有明白了当之理想。夫理想无统系，又不能透澈，则此理想未可谓为我所有也。有三道焉，可使一理想真成吾所自有：

一曰谈话 　友朋问答辩论，可使吾向所模糊了解者，今皆成明澈之言论。盖谈话非明白透澈不为功也。

二曰演说 　演说者，广义的谈话也。得一题，先集资料，次条理之，次融会贯通之，次以明白易晓之言抒达之；经此一番陶冶，此题真成吾所有矣。

三曰著作 　作文与演说同功，但此更耐久耳。即如吾所持“大同主义”（Cosmopolitanism or Internationalism），皆经十余次演说而来，始成一有统系的主义。今演说之日渐少，故有所触，辄记之此册（上所记甚零星细碎，然胜不记远矣），不独可以示他日之我，又可助此诸见解令真成我所自有之理想也。

早餐后，驱车进行美妙的旅行，穿越橡胶种植园的山丘驶向港口。漂亮的植物；看起来喜庆的中式别墅。远眺有着众多小岛的大海。另一群忠实的犹太人来到了船上。大约11点才起航，航行在美丽如画的绿色群岛间。

中国人能通过勤奋、节俭和子孙众多超越其他任何民族。新加坡几乎完全在他们手中。他们作为商人颇受敬重，远胜于被认为靠不住的日本人。要从心理上理解日本人可能很难；而我自从感觉完全理解不了日本歌曲后，也不敢再做尝试。昨天我又听到另一首歌，长得使我头晕目眩。
